meta.title: E9X M3 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M3 E90/E92/E93 engine — S65 4.0 V8 and S65 4.4 GTS/CRT. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M3 E90/E92/E93 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S65 engine typically costs £6,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the standard S65B40 4.0-litre sits at £6,000–£12,000, while the S65B44 4.4-litre in the GTS and CRT costs £8,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £910–£1,170. A full engine rebuild can exceed £17,000. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E9X M3 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S65B40 is the only engine available in the vast majority of E9X M3s. It's a high-revving masterpiece, but it has well-documented reliability concerns — rod bearing wear, throttle actuator failure, and VANOS cover cracking are the primary issues. It's not unreliable, but it requires diligent preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M3 E90/E92/E93?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S65 suffers from rod bearing wear — the single biggest concern, which can lead to catastrophic engine failure if ignored. Throttle actuators are prone to failure, causing limp mode. The plastic VANOS covers can crack, leading to oil starvation and engine damage. Cooling system components and oil leaks from valve covers are also common as these cars age.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M3 E90/E92/E93 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's a high-performance car with high-performance maintenance requirements. The S65 is fundamentally robust, but it requires diligent preventative maintenance — rod bearings, throttle actuators, and VANOS covers all need attention. Neglect is expensive; preventative maintenance is essential. If you can budget for the maintenance, it's one of the most rewarding M3s ever made.

mainEntity.name: Which E9X M3 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a car without documented rod bearing replacement is a significant risk. A neglected example without throttle actuator or VANOS cover history is also risky. Avoid any car with obvious knocking sounds or poor service history. The GTS and CRT are rare and collectable but have even higher running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E9X M3 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rod bearing knock (particularly on cold starts), sudden loss of power or limp mode (throttle actuators), oil pressure warnings (VANOS cover failure), or overheating (cooling system failure) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E9X M3 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £20,000 M3, a £6,000–£12,000 S65 recon is 30–60% — a serious but often viable decision for a cherished car. For a GTS at £50,000+, a £8,000–£15,000 S65B44 recon is 16–30% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. These are appreciating modern classics. If you're buying, budget for rod bearings immediately (£1,800–£2,500).

mainEntity.name: How long do S65 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper preventative maintenance, the S65 can exceed 100,000 miles. Rod bearings should be changed at around 60,000 miles (or immediately if you don't know the car's history). Throttle actuators and VANOS covers should be checked at the same time. Many well-maintained examples are now well over 100,000 miles and still going strong.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E90, E92, and E93 M3?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the same S65 V8 engine and mechanicals. The E90 is the 4-door saloon (2008–2011) — rarer and slightly stiffer than the coupé. The E92 is the 2-door coupé (2007–2013) — the most common and iconic body style. The E93 is the 2-door convertible (2007–2013) — the heaviest due to its folding hardtop, and the slowest as a result. The E92 GTS and E90 CRT are limited-production models with the 4.4-litre S65B44.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E9X M3 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For petrol: S65B40 (standard M3), S65B44 (M3 GTS), S65B44A (M3 CRT). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for the S65 engine family. The S65 is a high-revving masterpiece that won the International Engine of the Year Award for Best 3-litre to 4-litre Engine five years in a row (2008–2012). However, it has well-documented reliability concerns — rod bearing wear, throttle actuator failure, and VANOS cover cracking. The S65B44 found in the GTS and CRT is essentially the same engine with an enlarged stroke and is even rarer and more expensive to maintain.

overview.intro: The E9X M3 is the fourth generation of the BMW M3, produced from 2007 to 2013. It was the first and only M3 to feature a V8 engine — the S65 — marking a significant departure from the inline-four and inline-six engines of its predecessors. The S65 was derived from BMW's S85 V10 found in the E60 M5, sharing the same bore, stroke, and Double-VANOS system. The E9X M3 was offered in three body styles: the E90 saloon (2008–2011), E92 coupé (2007–2013), and E93 convertible (2007–2013). Limited-production models included the E92 M3 GTS (2010–2011) and E90 M3 CRT (2011–2012), both featuring the larger 4.4-litre S65B44 engine. The E9X M3 was succeeded by the turbocharged F80 M3 in 2014.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E90/E92/E93 (front-engine, rear-wheel drive) • Predecessor: BMW M3 (E46) • Successor: BMW M3 (F80) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E90 Saloon, E92 Coupé, E93 Convertible
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M3 nameplate generated significant UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with demand concentrated on the S65-powered E9X M3 based on market patterns. The S65 remains one of the most sought-after engines in the M3 lineage.

items.quote: As the only engine available in the vast majority of E9X M3s, it's the only realistic choice. Values are starting to climb as the last naturally-aspirated M3 gains classic status. Budget for rod bearings and throttle actuators, and you'll have one of the greatest driver's cars ever made.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the definitive E9X M3 experience and are prepared for the maintenance.

items.quote: The S65 is a high-performance engine with known vulnerabilities — rod bearing wear, throttle actuator failure, and VANOS cover cracking. Early cars (2007–2010) are more susceptible to these issues, and neglect can lead to catastrophic engine failure costing £12,000+.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with documented preventative maintenance or budget for immediate rod bearing replacement.

items.quote: The 4.4-litre S65 is even rarer and more expensive than the standard engine. A full engine replacement can exceed £15,000, and parts are increasingly difficult to source. The GTS and CRT are collector's items, but ownership costs are eye-watering.
items.whoItsFor: serious collectors with the budget to maintain a rare and appreciating asset.

items.quote: The 4.4-litre S65 is often forgotten because the GTS and CRT are so rare — only 135 GTS units and 67 CRT units were produced. It's the ultimate S65: 444 bhp, titanium exhaust, and motorsport heritage. A true collector's piece.
items.whoItsFor: serious BMW collectors who understand the rarity and value of these limited-edition models.

rows.ownershipVerdict: High Performance, High Cost — budget for preventative maintenance

economicsRule.title: The E9X M3 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£12,000 S65 recon against a £15,000–£30,000 M3 sits at 20–80% of the car's value — at the upper end, that brushes our 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to 30–60% — a serious but often viable decision for a cherished car. For a GTS at £50,000+, a £8,000–£15,000 S65B44 recon is 16–30% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. These are appreciating modern classics; the replacement cost is a manageable proportion of the vehicle's worth. But beware of rod bearings — budget for preventative work (£1,800–£2,500) if buying used.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E9X M3 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S65B40 (standard) — it's the only realistic option; budget for preventative maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E9X M3 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S65B44 (GTS/CRT) — rare parts, specialist labour, eye-watering costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E9X M3 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: S65B40 (standard) — the last naturally-aspirated M3, an appreciating modern classic.

problems.title: Rod Bearing Wear (All S65 Engines)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an E9X M3, budget for rod bearing replacement immediately if there's no documented history — £1,800–£2,500 is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows a failure. Many specialists recommend changing the bearings at around 100,000 km (62,000 miles) whether you hear the problem or not. On a £20,000+ car, this is a cost of ownership you should budget for.

problems.title: Throttle Actuator Failure (All S65 Engines)
problems.recommendation: If your M3 develops loss of power or enters limp mode, have the throttle actuators checked immediately — £800–£1,500 for replacement is cheaper than being stranded. Uprated throttle actuators with a lifetime warranty are available.

problems.title: VANOS Cover Cracking (All S65 Engines)
problems.recommendation: Many specialists recommend replacing the plastic VANOS covers with aluminium units as preventative maintenance — £500–£1,200 is cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows oil starvation.

problems.title: Cooling System & Oil Leaks (All S65 Engines)
problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system and valve covers inspected at every service — a £400–£1,000 preventative repair is cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

replacementCosts.h2: E9X M3 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E9X M3 only. The standard M3 used the S65B40 4.0-litre V8. The GTS and CRT used the S65B44 4.4-litre V8 and were limited-production models. The E92 M3 GTS was produced in 2010–2011 (135 units), and the E90 M3 CRT in 2011–2012 (67 units). The F80 M3 (2014–2020) used a twin-turbo S55 inline-six and is covered on its own Generation Page.
